candidate_problems.text: How can 'institutional veto density' be defined and measured in a way that distinguishes it from related factors like federalism, bicameralism, judicial review, party fragmentation, or simple policy complexity? The conjecture is strong only if veto density becomes an explanatory variable with independent predictive power across cases, rather than a relabeling of general institutional friction.

What determines the threshold at which additional public support stops mattering and structural vetoes become decisive? The conjecture implies a nonlinear regime change: beyond some point, more agreement yields little marginal effect unless transition costs or choke points are altered. Identifying when preference strength can still overcome veto density, and when it cannot, is a major unresolved question.

Under what conditions do anti-domination institutions remain welfare-improving safeguards versus becoming pathological brakes on legitimate reform? The conjecture traces current resistance to historical designs meant to prevent capture and instability, but leaves open the core tradeoff: how to reduce veto power without increasing risks of domination, rash policy swings, or regime fragility.
